prohibited.label: Prohibit all
prohibited.description: Prohibition of any use of AI, with no exceptions.

prohibited_except_assisted_research.label: Prohibit except for Westlaw, Lexis, etc.
prohibited_except_assisted_research.description: The order/rule prohibits litigants from using AI with an exception for legal tools such as Westlaw and Lexis.

disclosure_with_traditional_verification.label: Permit with verification via traditional resources
disclosure_with_traditional_verification.description: The order/rule permits litigants to use AI as long as they verify the accuracy of any AI-generated content using "print reporters" or "traditional legal databases" (or "other reliable means" or similar language).

disclosure_required.label: Permit with disclosure/certification
disclosure_required.description: The order/rule permits litigants to use any AI, but litigants must disclose their AI usage and certify that they have reviewed and verified any AI-generated content. The order/rule does not specify exactly how litigants must verify the AI-generated content.

disclosure_except_assisted_research.label: Permit with disclosure/certification for non-Westlaw, Lexis, etc.
disclosure_except_assisted_research.description: The order/rule permits litigants to use AI and requires them to disclose and certify their AI usage. However, the disclosure and certification requirement does not apply to the use of legal tools such as Westlaw or Lexis.

permitted_with_caution.label: Permit with general accuracy obligations
permitted_with_caution.description: The order/rule permits litigants to use any AI. It may remind them of their obligations under FRCP Rule 11 or to verify the accuracy of any AI-generated content, but it does not require that litigants explicitly disclose and certify AI usage.

no_explicit_rule.label: No explicit AI rule
no_explicit_rule.description: Court or judge has standing orders or local rules but none specifically address AI.

permitted.label: Permitted with no qualifications
permitted.description: AI is permitted, with no other conditions or reminders. This category is quite rare.

guidance_for_courts.label: Guidance for courts
guidance_for_courts.description: AI guidance, policies, or reports directed at judges, judicial staff, and court operations — NOT at litigants or attorneys filing documents.

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois
rules.title: Standing Order for Civil Cases Before Magistrate Judge Fuentes
rules.summary: "The Court refers all counsel to the Illinois Supreme Court's policy on artificial intelligence use, effective January 1, 2025. The Court expects that no counsel will submit hallucinated authorities and that all counsel will follow Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. ... The Court will continue to presume that the preexisting Rule 11 certification is a representation by filers, as living, breathing, thinking human beings, that they themselves have read and analyzed all cited authorities to ensure that such authorities actually exist and that the filings comply with Rule 11(b)(2)." The referenced Illinois Supreme Court policy states: "The use of AI by litigants, attorneys, judges, judicial clerks, research attorneys, and court staff providing similar support may be expected, should not be discouraged, and is authorized, provided it complies with legal and ethical standards. Disclosure of AI use should not be required in a pleading."
rules.verbatim_key_language: The Court refers all counsel to the Illinois Supreme Court's policy on artificial intelligence use, effective January 1, 2025. The Court expects that no counsel will submit hallucinated authorities and that all counsel will follow Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.
rules.provenance: manually verified 2026-04-17; adopts Illinois Supreme Court AI policy (https://ilcourtsaudio.blob.core.windows.net/antilles-resources/resources/e43964ab-8874-4b7a-be4e-63af019cb6f7/Illinois%20Supreme%20Court%20AI%20Policy.pdf) which authorizes AI use and states disclosure should not be required

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania
rules.title: Standing Order Re: Use of Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Requires counsel to disclose any use of AI in preparing a filing and to certify that the accuracy of all citations and legal arguments has been verified.
rules.provenance: manually verified 2026-04-17; Baylson is now a senior judge (not active); standing order PDF confirmed on court website

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois
rules.title: Standing Order Regarding Professional Responsibility and AI
rules.summary: Reminds attorneys of their duties under Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 11(b) and 26(g) without imposing additional disclosure or certification requirements. Takes a hands-off approach emphasizing existing professional responsibility rules. See "Artificial Intelligence (AI)" under Case Procedures on the judge's page.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified at judge's page with AI dropdown section

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Western District of North Carolina
rules.title: Standing Order In Re: Use of Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "[B]riefs or memoranda submitted to the court must have a certification that no artificial intelligence was used during the research to prepare the document except for that in the standard online legal research sources Bloomberg, FastCase, Lexis or Westlaw." Requires attorney or paralegal verification of every statement and citation for accuracy.
rules.verbatim_key_language: briefs or memoranda submitted to the court must have a certification that no artificial intelligence was used during the research to prepare the document except for that in the standard online legal research sources
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Ohio
rules.title: Standing Order on the Use of Generative AI
rules.summary: Prohibits use of AI in the preparation of any filing submitted to the Court, with exception for information gathered from legal search engines such as Westlaw or LexisNexis, or Internet search engines such as Google or Bing. Violations may result in sanctions including striking pleadings, economic sanctions, contempt, or dismissal.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania
rules.title: Standing Order Re: Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "[C]ounsel (or a party representing himself or herself) [must] disclose any use of generative Artificial Intelligence ('AI') in the preparation of any complaint, answer, motion, brief, or other paper filed with the Court... [and] certify that each and every citation to the law or the record in the filing has been verified as authentic and accurate."
rules.verbatim_key_language: counsel (or a party representing himself or herself) [must] disclose any use of generative Artificial Intelligence ('AI') in the preparation of any complaint, answer, motion, brief, or other paper filed with the Court
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Texas
rules.title: General Order 2025-04: Use of Generative AI in Court Filings
rules.summary: Requires attorneys to exercise independent legal judgment and thoroughly review any filing prepared with the assistance of generative AI before submission. Emphasizes that attorneys have a non-delegable responsibility to verify all content in their filings, regardless of whether generative AI was used in preparation.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Texas
rules.title: Local Rule CV-11(g): Generative AI Verification Requirement (Amended)
rules.summary: Amended rule requiring all litigants (not just pro se) to review and verify the accuracy and quality of information produced by AI technology that is used in a filing. Expands 2023 rule that only applied to pro se litigants after the court observed increasing numbers of filings that improperly utilized generative AI.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from General Order 25-07

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas
rules.title: Local Civil Rule 7.2(f): Disclosure of Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "A brief prepared using generative artificial intelligence must disclose this fact on the first page under the heading 'Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence.' ... A party who files a brief that does not contain the disclosure required by subsection (f)(1) of this rule certifies that no part of the brief was prepared using generative artificial intelligence."
rules.verbatim_key_language: A brief prepared using generative artificial intelligence must disclose this fact on the first page under the heading 'Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence.'
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from CIVRULES.pdf (effective Sept. 2, 2025 version); supersedes Judge Starr's individual standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California
rules.title: Civil Standing Order (Updated 2025)
rules.summary: "Documents created with [generative AI] must be identified in the title, caption, in a table, or by separate notice... AI-hallucinated citations or fictitious legal citations are prohibited."
rules.verbatim_key_language: Documents created with [generative AI] must be identified in the title, caption, in a table, or by separate notice
rules.provenance: user-verified URLs 2026-04-18; quote from standing order PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois
rules.title: Standing Order Prohibiting AI Use in Drafting
rules.summary: Parties may not use Artificial Intelligence to draft their memoranda or as authority to support their motions. Complete prohibition on AI use in filings. See "Memorandum of Law Requirements" under Case Procedures on the judge's page.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court website searches

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Central District of California
rules.title: Standing Order for Civil Cases — AI Disclosure Required
rules.summary: "Any party who uses generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) to generate any portion of a brief, pleading, or other filing must attach to the filing a separate declaration disclosing the use of artificial intelligence and certifying that the filer has reviewed the source material and verified that the artificially generated content is accurate and complies with the filer's Rule 11 obligations."
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any party who uses generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) to generate any portion of a brief, pleading, or other filing must attach to the filing a separate declaration disclosing the use of artificial intelligence and certifying that the filer has reviewed the source material and verified that the artificially generated content is accurate and complies with the filer's Rule 11 obligations.
rules.provenance: user-verified URL 2026-04-18; corrected category from permitted to disclosure_required per actual order text

rules.court: Florida Sixth Judicial Circuit Court, Section 22, Family Court Division
rules.title: Standing Order on AI Use in Family Court Filings
rules.summary: "I certify that I have personally reviewed this filing or submission, verified the accuracy of all legal authorities and factual assertions through traditional methods, and conducted a reasonable inquiry into the truth and accuracy of all statements herein." Mandatory certification required on every filing, immediately above the signature line.
rules.verbatim_key_language: verified the accuracy of all legal authorities and factual assertions through traditional methods
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; notable for strong prohibition language

rules.court: California Judicial Council (Statewide)
rules.title: Rule 10.430: Generative Artificial Intelligence Use Policies
rules.summary: "[R]equires disclosure of the use of or reliance on generative AI if the final version of a written, visual, or audio work provided to the public consists entirely of generative AI outputs... [and] prohibit[s] entry of confidential or nonpublic information into public AI systems." First statewide court system framework in the nation. Applies to all California state courts.
rules.verbatim_key_language: requires disclosure of the use of or reliance on generative AI if the final version of a written, visual, or audio work provided to the public consists entirely of generative AI outputs
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; landmark statewide policy, first in U.S.

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado
rules.title: Standing Order Regarding Artificial Intelligence Certification
rules.summary: Requires disclosure when AI is used and certification that all content has been verified for accuracy. Judge Wang has actively enforced this order, issuing an Order to Show Cause in *Coomer v. Lindell* after identifying nearly thirty defective citations in a brief submitted by attorneys who admitted to using generative AI without cite-checking.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; notable for active enforcement through sanctions

rules.court: U.S. Court of International Trade
rules.title: Order on Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "[A]ny submission containing text drafted with the assistance of a generative artificial intelligence program... must be accompanied by: (1) A disclosure notice that identifies the program used and the specific portion of text that have been so drafted; (2) A certification that the use of such program has not resulted in the disclosure of any confidential or business proprietary information to any unauthorized party."
rules.verbatim_key_language: any submission containing text drafted with the assistance of a generative artificial intelligence program... must be accompanied by: (1) A disclosure notice that identifies the program used and the specific portion of text that have been so drafted; (2) A certification that the use of such program has not resulted in the disclosure of any confidential or business proprietary information
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; second CIT judge order following Vaden

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Hawaii
rules.title: Disclosure and Certification Requirements - Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Requires disclosure if generative AI is utilized in the preparation of any documents filed with the Court. The guidelines are consistent with Rule 11(b) of the Federal Rules and require certain disclosure and certification requirements when AI is used.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; mirrors Starr-style prototype AI standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of New Jersey
rules.title: General Pretrial and Trial Procedures - Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "The use of any generative AI (such as OpenAI's ChatGPT or Google's Bard) in court filings requires a mandatory disclosure/certification that: (1) identifies the GAI program; (2) identifies the portion of the filing drafted by GAI; and (3) certifies that the GAI work product was diligently reviewed by a human being for accuracy and applicability." Failure to comply may result in sanctions.
rules.verbatim_key_language: The use of any generative AI (such as OpenAI's ChatGPT or Google's Bard) in court filings requires a mandatory disclosure/certification that: (1) identifies the GAI program; (2) identifies the portion of the filing drafted by GAI; and (3) certifies that the GAI work product was diligently reviewed by a human being for accuracy and applicability
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; proactive amendment to pretrial procedures in late 2023

rules.court: U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of California
rules.title: General Order 210: Filings Using Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Court-wide general order governing the use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) for all pleadings, motions, and papers filed in Court proceedings. Establishes requirements and guidelines for attorneys and parties who use generative AI tools when preparing court filings in bankruptcy proceedings.
rules.provenance: user-verified PDF URL 2026-04-19; General Order 210

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Nebraska
rules.title: NECivR 7.0.1: Certificate Regarding Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: District-wide local rule requiring a certificate stating either that no generative AI was used, or that a human verified all AI-generated text including citations. Nebraska is one of only two federal districts (along with Hawaii) with a district-wide AI disclosure requirement.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; one of two federal districts with district-wide requirement

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Montana
rules.title: Pro Hac Vice Admission Order — AI Prohibition
rules.summary: "Use of artificial intelligence automated drafting programs, such as Chat GPT, is prohibited." This prohibition is limited to counsel appearing pro hac vice (out-of-state attorneys seeking temporary admission to practice in Montana federal court), making it narrower in scope than most AI standing orders.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Use of artificial intelligence automated drafting programs, such as Chat GPT, is prohibited
rules.provenance: user-verified PDF URL 2026-04-19 via CourtListener; note: pro hac vice admission order (not a general standing order); PDF requires OCR

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Western District of Oklahoma
rules.title: Disclosure and Certification Requirements - Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Requires parties that used generative AI to draft or prepare a court filing to disclose "that [G]AI was used and the specific [G]AI tool that was used." Also requires attorneys and unrepresented parties to "certify in the document that the person has checked the accuracy of any portion of the document drafted by [G]AI, including all citations and legal authority."
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; mirrors Starr prototype, adopted by Judge Robertson in E.D. Okla.; user-verified PDF URL 2026-04-19

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Central District of California
rules.title: Civil Standing Order — Artificial Intelligence (AI)
rules.summary: "Use of ChatGPT or other such tools is not prohibited, but counsel must at all times personally confirm for themselves the accuracy of any content generated by these tools. ... Any submission containing AI-generated content must include a certification that lead trial counsel has personally verified the content's accuracy. Failure to include this certification or comply with this verification requirement will be grounds for sanctions. Counsel is responsible for maintaining records of all prompts or inquiries."
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any submission containing AI-generated content must include a certification that lead trial counsel has personally verified the content's accuracy. Failure to include this certification or comply with this verification requirement will be grounds for sanctions.
rules.provenance: user-verified PDF URL 2026-04-18; corrected court domain from cand to proper N.D. Cal. judge page

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California
rules.title: Civil Standing Order — Use of Generative AI
rules.summary: Use of generative AI in drafting filings is "not prohibited," but attorneys must personally verify the accuracy of any research conducted by such means. "[C]ounsel alone bears ethical responsibility for all statements made in filings," regardless of whether an AI tool was involved. Reinforces existing duties (competence, candor, Rule 11) without an affirmative disclosure requirement.
rules.provenance: user-verified PDF URL 2026-04-18; standing order PDF dated 2026-03-16

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Ohio
rules.title: Standing Order on Artificial Intelligence Use
rules.summary: "The Court reminds all parties that they are not allowed to use AI—for any purpose—to prepare any filings in the instant case or any case before the undersigned." However, the order carves out an exception for "AI in legal and internet search engines." "The penalty for violating this provision includes, inter alia, striking the pleading from the record, the imposition of economic sanctions or contempt, and dismissal of the lawsuit."
rules.verbatim_key_language: The Court reminds all parties that they are not allowed to use AI—for any purpose—to prepare any filings in the instant case or any case before the undersigned
rules.provenance: user-verified PDF URL 2026-04-18; PDF has AI policy on page 3

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Kansas
rules.title: Standing Order 26-01: Use of AI
rules.summary: Reminds litigants that they "are responsible for the content of their own filings and for complying with all applicable procedural rules and duties of candor even when they use AI to generate all or portions of court filings." Notes that AI tools can produce false statements of fact or law, including citations to fabricated or incorrect legal authority. Does not require affirmative disclosure of AI use.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; court-wide standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Georgia
rules.title: Use of Artificial Intelligence by Pro Se Litigants
rules.summary: Pro se parties are strongly encouraged to be cautious when using AI tools such as ChatGPT. AI does not alleviate a party's responsibility for making sure that all facts and legal authorities cited in court filings are accurate. Failure to do so may result in sanctions under Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, which could include dismissal of the lawsuit. Guidance document for self-represented litigants, not a mandatory order.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; pro se guidance document, not a binding order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri
rules.title: Artificial Intelligence Guidance for Attorneys and Pro Se Parties
rules.summary: Attorneys and self-represented parties acknowledge they will be held responsible for document contents, including any portion generated with artificial intelligence. Emphasizes existing professional responsibility obligations without requiring explicit disclosure.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; guidance page on court website

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of New Mexico
rules.title: Standing Order Regarding Use of Generative AI
rules.summary: Requires disclosure when AI was used and identification of the specific AI tool that was used. Unrepresented parties or attorneys must certify in the document that they have checked the accuracy of any portion generated by AI. Judge Strickland standing order.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; user correction 2026-04-19 — PDF attributes to Judge Margaret I. Strickland (not court-wide)

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York
rules.title: Civil Rules & Practices — AI Disclosure Requirement
rules.summary: Any party who utilizes generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools must disclose that AI has been used, and must certify that they have independently reviewed and verified the accuracy of any portion of the document drafted by generative AI, including all citations, quotations, and legal authority. Failure to comply may result in sanctions, the document being stricken, or other remedies. A model certification is available on the Court's website.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; individual judge standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York
rules.title: Individual Rules and Practices in Civil Cases — AI Disclosure
rules.summary: "Any party who uses generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) must attach a separate declaration disclosing the use of artificial intelligence and certifying that the filer has reviewed the source material and verified that the artificially generated content is accurate and complies with the filer's Rule 11 obligations."
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any party who uses generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) must attach a separate declaration disclosing the use of artificial intelligence and certifying that the filer has reviewed the source material and verified that the artificially generated content is accurate and complies with the filer's Rule 11 obligations
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; individual judge standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York
rules.title: Individual Rules and Practices for Civil Cases — AI Certification
rules.summary: If any attorney or pro se litigant uses an artificial intelligence tool, the filing must include a signed certification explaining how the tool was used, stating whether they personally reviewed the filing for accuracy, and describing in detail the steps taken to verify the accuracy of all legal authorities and factual assertions generated by the tool.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; magistrate judge individual standing order; user correction 2026-04-20: first name is Henry not Hector

rules.court: U.S. Court of International Trade
rules.title: Order on Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Requires documents drafted with assistance from generative AI programs like ChatGPT or Google Bard to include a disclosure notice identifying the program and specific text portions drafted by AI, plus a certification that no confidential information was disclosed to unauthorized parties. Multiple CIT judges have issued similar orders.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; user correction 2026-04-19 — PDF filename Order_on_Artificial_Intelligence-Judge_Kelly.pdf attributes to Claire R. Kelly, not Katzmann

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Ohio
rules.title: Standing Order Governing the Use of Generative AI
rules.summary: Court-wide standing order governing use of generative AI. Specific requirements not accessible in search results, but appears to be a disclosure/verification requirement similar to other district-wide orders.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; user correction 2026-04-19 — judge attribution confirmed as Jeffery P. Hopkins

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia
rules.title: Civil Standing Order — AI Disclosure Requirement
rules.summary: "All counsel and pro se parties must disclose the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in any capacity to prepare documents submitted to the Court." Additionally, "Arguments in briefs to the Court which are supported by AI-generated caselaw (i.e., cases that do not actually exist) are not acceptable." Counsel and pro se parties shall not input, upload, or otherwise expose any information subject to a protective order into any AI program. Failure to comply may result in sanctions including dismissal or entry of default.
rules.verbatim_key_language: All counsel and pro se parties must disclose the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in any capacity to prepare documents submitted to the Court.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; confirmed from TRJ_CVStandingOrder.pdf; Judge Johnson confirmed by U.S. Senate 2024

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia
rules.title: Standing Case Instructions — AI Protected Information Restriction
rules.summary: "Counsel and pro se parties shall not input, upload, or otherwise expose any information subject to a protective order into any artificial intelligence program, tool or application. Failure to comply with this rule may result in appropriate sanctions, up to and including dismissal or entry of default." Does not require affirmative disclosure of AI use; focuses on protecting confidential information.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Counsel and pro se parties shall not input, upload, or otherwise expose any information subject to a protective order into any artificial intelligence program, tool or application.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; confirmed from SCJ_Standing_Case_Instructions.pdf dated October 15, 2025

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Central District of California
rules.title: Civil Standing Order - AI Disclosure Requirement
rules.summary: "Any party who uses generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) to generate any portion of a brief, pleading, or other filing must attach to the filing a separate declaration disclosing the use of artificial intelligence and certifying that the filer has reviewed the source material and verified that the artificially generated content is accurate and complies with the filer's Rule 11 obligations."
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any party who uses generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) to generate any portion of a brief, pleading, or other filing must attach to the filing a separate declaration disclosing the use of artificial intelligence and certifying that the filer has reviewed the source material and verified that the artificially generated content is accurate and complies with the filer's Rule 11 obligations.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; individual judge standing order from C.D. Cal.

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Central District of California
rules.title: Standing Order for Civil Cases - AI Disclosure Requirement
rules.summary: "Any party who uses generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) to generate any portion of a brief, pleading, or other filing must attach to the filing a separate declaration disclosing the use of artificial intelligence and certifying that the filer has reviewed the source material and verified that the artificially generated content is accurate and complies with the filer's Rule 11 obligations."
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any party who uses generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel, or Google Bard) to generate any portion of a brief, pleading, or other filing must attach to the filing a separate declaration disclosing the use of artificial intelligence and certifying that the filer has reviewed the source material and verified that the artificially generated content is accurate and complies with the filer's Rule 11 obligations.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; magistrate judge individual standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York
rules.title: Individual Rules and Practices in Civil Cases - AI Accuracy Requirement
rules.summary: "[L]itigants [are] responsible for verifying the accuracy of any output produced in whole or in part by an AI tool." Does not require affirmative disclosure of AI use, but emphasizes responsibility for accurate filings. Model certification for AI use available on Court's website.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; individual judge standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York
rules.title: Individual Rules and Practices in Civil Cases - AI Verification Requirement
rules.summary: "The Court takes no position on the use of artificial intelligence... but requires counsel to personally confirm the accuracy of any research conducted by these means at all times." Reminds counsel of existing obligations under Rule 11 without requiring explicit disclosure of AI use.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; individual judge standing order

rules.court: Florida Eleventh Judicial Circuit (Miami-Dade County)
rules.title: Administrative Order No. 26-04: Disclosure of Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "Any attorney or self-represented litigant who uses any generative artificial intelligence tool in the preparation of a pleading, motion, memorandum, response, proposed order, or other court record, must disclose such use on the face of the filing." Required certification language: "Generative artificial intelligence was used in the preparation of this filing. The undersigned certifies that all factual assertions, legal authority, and citations have been independently reviewed and verified for accuracy and accepts full responsibility for the contents of this filing."
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any attorney or self-represented litigant who uses any generative artificial intelligence tool in the preparation of a pleading, motion, memorandum, response, proposed order, or other court record, must disclose such use on the face of the filing.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-19; Administrative Order No. 26-04 issued by Chief Judge Ariana Fajardo Orshan covering Miami-Dade County

rules.court: Florida Seventeenth Judicial Circuit (Broward County)
rules.title: Administrative Order 2026-03-Gen: Use of Artificial Intelligence in Court Filings
rules.summary: "When AI has been used or assisted in the use of preparation, researching, drafting pleadings, drafting documents, filing documents, and/or discovery requests, the document shall identify the specific tool used in such manner." Required certification: "The undersigned herby certifies that generative artificial intelligence was used to prepare this [TITLE OF DOCUMENT BEING FILED]. The undersigned has independently verified the accuracy of every citation to the law and /or the record, and the accuracy of any language drafted by generative artificial intelligence, including quotations, citations, paraphrased assertions, facts, and legal analysis."
rules.verbatim_key_language: When AI has been used or assisted in the use of preparation, researching, drafting pleadings, drafting documents, filing documents, and/or discovery requests, the document shall identify the specific tool used in such manner.
rules.provenance: user-provided PDF URL 2026-04-20

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois
rules.title: Standing Order on AI Use Disclosure Requirement
rules.summary: "Any party using AI in the preparation of materials submitted to the court must disclose in the filing that an AI tool was used to conduct legal research and/or was used in any way in the preparation of the submitted document." Order cautions that relying on an AI tool won't excuse a failure to make a reasonable inquiry under Rule 11. Notably broader than some other orders as it applies to AI used for research purposes, not just drafting.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any party using AI in the preparation of materials submitted to the court must disclose in the filing that an AI tool was used to conduct legal research and/or was used in any way in the preparation of the submitted document
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; AI disclosure section on judge's case procedures page; requirement covers both research and drafting use of AI tools

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York
rules.title: Individual Practices in Civil Cases — AI Accuracy Requirement
rules.summary: "Use of ChatGPT or other such tools is not prohibited, but counsel must at all times personally confirm for themselves the accuracy of any research conducted by these means." Counsel is responsible for providing complete and accurate representations of the record, procedural history, and legal authorities. Does not require affirmative disclosure of AI use; emphasizes existing professional responsibility obligations.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Use of ChatGPT or other such tools is not prohibited, but counsel must at all times personally confirm for themselves the accuracy of any research conducted by these means
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; individual practices in civil cases PDF revised March 14, 2025; minimally restrictive approach relying on existing rules

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania
rules.title: Standing Order on Artificial Intelligence Use
rules.summary: "If any attorney for a party, or a pro se party, has used generative Artificial Intelligence ('AI')—including but not limited to ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, or any other program that uses machine learning to create new content—in a citation of any legal authority filed with the Court, and assigned to Judge Kai N. Scott, then they MUST, in a clear and plain factual statement, disclose that generative AI has been used to assist with the citation of legal authority, disclose what specific generative AI program was used, and CERTIFY that each and every citation of legal authority has been verified as accurate."
rules.verbatim_key_language: if any attorney for a party, or a pro se party, has used generative Artificial Intelligence ('AI')—including but not limited to ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, or any other program that uses machine learning to create new content—in a citation of any legal authority filed with the Court, and assigned to Judge Kai N. Scott, then they MUST, in a clear and plain factual statement, disclose that generative AI has been used
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; standing order issued March 3, 2025; Judge Scott confirmed to E.D. Pa. in 2022; has also ordered sanctions against attorneys for citing hallucinated cases

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania
rules.title: Certificate of Use of Generative AI Requirement
rules.summary: Any party using generative AI tools (including ChatGPT or Google's Bard) in preparing documents must include a Certificate of Use of Generative AI disclosing the use and certifying that a person has checked the accuracy of any AI-generated portions of the document, including all citations and legal authority. Addresses risks of legally or factually incorrect information, nonexistent legal citations, or disclosure of confidential information.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Magistrate Judge Caraballo's individual judicial preferences require Certificate of Use of Generative AI

rules.court: U.S. Court of International Trade
rules.title: Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Practice Before the Court
rules.summary: Court-wide guidance stating that attorneys using generative artificial intelligence should be mindful of individual chambers procedures regarding confidential or business proprietary information and Rule 11 obligations regarding attorneys' representations to the Court. Does not require affirmative disclosure; emphasizes existing professional responsibility obligations.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; court-wide guidance published March 21, 2025; supplements individual judge standing orders from Restani and Kelly

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia
rules.title: Guidelines to Parties and Counsel in Civil Cases Proceeding Before the Honorable Tiffany R. Johnson
rules.summary: Requires all counsel and pro se parties to disclose any use of artificial intelligence in preparing documents submitted to the Court. If AI was used, parties must sign and file a Disclosure of Use of Artificial Intelligence, certifying under penalty of perjury that they have independently reviewed the document to confirm accuracy, legitimacy, and use of good and applicable law pursuant to Rule 11 FRCP. Failure to comply may result in sanctions including dismissal or default judgment.
rules.verbatim_key_language: All counsel and pro se parties must disclose the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in any capacity to prepare documents submitted to the Court. If AI was used in any way to prepare a filing or submission to the Court or chambers, counsel and/or pro se parties are REQUIRED to sign and file a Disclosure of Use of Artificial Intelligence stating, as follows: "This document was generated with the assistance of [identify AI tool name]. I hereby certify under penalty of perjury that, despite reliance on an AI tool, I have independently reviewed this document to confirm accuracy, legitimacy, and use of good and applicable law, pursuant to Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure."
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; PDF verified at gand.uscourts.gov; standing order undated but current; Note: queue item listed 'Thomas R. Jones' but PDF signature confirms judge is Tiffany R. Johnson (TRJ)

rules.court: Arizona Supreme Court
rules.title: Generative AI: Ethical Best Practices for Lawyers and Judges
rules.summary: The Arizona Steering Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts issued ethical best practices guidance for lawyers and judges using generative AI. The guidance emphasizes that all ethical obligations still apply when using AI, covering: competence and diligence, confidentiality, client communication, billing, court candor, bias/discrimination, and supervision. Does not require affirmative disclosure to courts, but reminds legal professionals they must verify all AI-generated content, not enter confidential information into tools without proper safeguards, and cannot bill for time saved by AI.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Although the ethical rules do not currently require disclosure to the court that an attorney has used AI in connection with the litigation, certain courts' general, standing, and case-specific orders require it. ... Legal professionals must also check the work product to ensure that any factual or legal information is accurate, relevant, and otherwise appropriate. Because generative AI can err – and indeed it cannot even think – legal professionals must not defer to its judgment.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Arizona Supreme Court Steering Committee on AI published November 14, 2024; ethical guidance document for state

rules.court: Arkansas Supreme Court
rules.title: Administrative Order No. 25: Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Arkansas Supreme Court Administrative Order No. 25 addresses the use of artificial intelligence in legal documents, warning that uploading confidential or sealed information into generative AI tools may violate existing court rules governing document handling and professional conduct. The order warns of risks associated with AI-generated citations that may be 'hallucinated' and emphasizes that use of AI could violate Arkansas law or court rules if not done properly.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Arkansas Supreme Court case 2025 Ark. 117 creating Administrative Order No. 25; issued June 2025 per news reports

rules.court: Connecticut Judicial Branch
rules.title: Responsible AI Framework
rules.summary: Connecticut Judicial Branch released Responsible AI Framework on February 1, 2024, establishing policies for procurement, implementation, and ongoing assessment of AI systems. Requires divisions considering AI to submit documentation to the Judicial Branch's Artificial Intelligence Committee. Prohibits AI systems that result in unlawful discrimination or disparate impact. Framework includes AI determination characteristics, intake and inventory, impact assessment procedures, and procurement guidelines.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Connecticut Judicial Branch Responsible AI Framework effective February 1, 2024

rules.court: Florida Supreme Court / Florida Bar
rules.title: Florida Bar Ethics Opinion 24-1: Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Florida Bar Board of Governors unanimously approved Ethics Opinion 24-1 on January 19, 2024, providing guidance on ethical use of generative AI in legal practice. Addresses competence, confidentiality, and candor obligations. Lawyers must maintain legal expertise and develop reasonable understanding of AI technologies, conduct due diligence in selecting AI tools, and maintain transparency. Florida Supreme Court subsequently amended Rules Regulating the Florida Bar in Opinion No. SC2024-0032, effective October 28, 2024, addressing professional competence, client confidentiality, and supervision related to AI use.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Florida Bar Ethics Opinion 24-1 approved January 19, 2024; Florida Supreme Court amendments effective October 28, 2024

rules.court: Illinois Supreme Court
rules.title: Illinois Supreme Court Policy on Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: The use of AI by litigants, attorneys, judges, judicial clerks, research attorneys, and court staff may be expected, should not be discouraged, and is authorized provided it complies with legal and ethical standards. Disclosure of AI use should not be required in a pleading. Rules of Professional Conduct and Code of Judicial Conduct apply fully. All users must thoroughly review AI-generated content before submitting it to ensure accuracy and compliance. Judges remain ultimately responsible for their decisions irrespective of technological advancements.
rules.verbatim_key_language: The use of AI by litigants, attorneys, judges, judicial clerks, research attorneys, and court staff providing similar support may be expected, should not be discouraged, and is authorized provided it complies with legal and ethical standards. Disclosure of AI use should not be required in a pleading.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Illinois Supreme Court Policy effective January 1, 2025

rules.court: Georgia Judicial Council / Supreme Court
rules.title: Judicial Council Ad Hoc Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts Report
rules.summary: Georgia Supreme Court Chief Justice Michael P. Boggs established Ad Hoc Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts in October 2024, in partnership with National Center for State Courts. Committee chaired by Justice Andrew A. Pinson submitted report in July 2025 assessing risks and benefits of generative AI in courts. Report examines impact on evidence rules, civil and criminal procedure, adequacy of ethical and professional standards for lawyer competency, and need for standards for technology vendors.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Georgia Judicial Council committee report submitted July 2025

rules.court: Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court
rules.title: Interim Guidelines for Generative AI Use by Judges and Court Personnel
rules.summary: Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court Chief Justice Kimberly Budd announced interim guidelines for how judges and court personnel should use generative AI in December 2025 State of the Judiciary speech. GenAI currently only used for administrative tasks, with only public information allowed. AI tools cannot retain or use any entered information to train programs. Guidelines will be updated as the judiciary learns more about GenAI.
rules.provenance: user-provided URL 2026-04-20

rules.court: Michigan Judicial Council
rules.title: Generative AI and the Courts Workgroup Report and Recommendations
rules.summary: Michigan Judicial Council Generative AI Workgroup released comprehensive report in 2024 addressing use of AI in Michigan's judicial system. Report emphasizes establishing boundaries and ethical guidelines for AI use, referencing Michigan Ethics Opinion JI-155 on judicial AI use. Identifies challenges including implementation costs, need for training, and public skepticism. Report provides recommendations for balancing innovation with ethical considerations and public trust.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Michigan Judicial Council workgroup report released 2024

rules.court: New Jersey Supreme Court
rules.title: New Jersey Supreme Court Preliminary Guidelines on Use of Artificial Intelligence by Judiciary
rules.summary: New Jersey Supreme Court issued preliminary guidelines in January 2024 for judiciary use of artificial intelligence. Guidelines address ethical considerations including confidentiality, competence, and impartiality. Judges and staff may use AI tools for research and administrative tasks but must verify accuracy and maintain human oversight. Confidential case information may not be entered into publicly available AI systems.
rules.provenance: user-provided PDF URL 2026-04-20; March 2026 notice

rules.court: New York Unified Court System
rules.title: Interim Policy on Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence in the New York State Unified Court System
rules.summary: New York Unified Court System issued interim policy on generative AI use in October 2025. Policy permits judges and court personnel to use AI for research, drafting, and administrative tasks with appropriate safeguards. Users must verify AI outputs for accuracy, maintain confidentiality of case information, and exercise human judgment. Policy prohibits entering confidential information into public AI systems and requires disclosure when AI-generated content is used in court documents.
rules.provenance: user-provided PDF URL 2026-04-20

rules.court: Indiana Supreme Court
rules.title: Indiana Supreme Court AI Governance Committee Model Policies for Trial Courts
rules.summary: Indiana Supreme Court AI Governance Committee released model policies and toolkit for trial courts in February 2026. Supreme Court approved foundational policy for court staff and distributed bench card, checklist, model policies, and buyer's guide to trial courts. Model policies provide beginner, intermediate, and advanced levels of AI adoption with key principles of transparency, accountability, awareness, and confidentiality. Policy distinguishes between open and closed AI models, prohibits entering confidential information into publicly available AI systems, and requires judges and staff to verify accuracy of AI outputs while maintaining human oversight and independent judgment.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Indiana Supreme Court AI Governance Committee toolkit released February 2026

rules.court: Supreme Court of Virginia
rules.title: Legal Ethics Opinion 1901 — Use of Generative AI
rules.summary: Supreme Court of Virginia issued Rules for Generative AI Use effective June 6, 2024, last amended May 7, 2025. Rules apply to all judicial system personnel and outline authorized use of generative AI tools. Users must verify accuracy and completeness of AI-generated content, complete compliance training, and obtain pre-approval for non-vetted tools. Rules prohibit entering confidential or case-related information into non-vetted AI systems. Vetted tools include Lexis+ AI and Westlaw Precision. Users must activate opt-out features when available to prevent data collection. Rules emphasize AI systems cannot make decisions without human decision-maker involvement, particularly in criminal matters per Virginia Code Section 19.2-11.14.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any user of generative AI tools is responsible for understanding the limitations of any AI-generated content and verifying the accuracy and completeness of any AI-generated content. AI-generated content must not be relied upon as a sole source of reference or assumed to be truthful, credible, or unbiased.
rules.provenance: user-provided PDF URL 2026-04-20; LEO 1901 is the only thing user could find on AI for litigants

rules.court: Supreme Court of South Carolina
rules.title: Interim Policy on the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Chief Justice Kittredge issued interim policy on March 25, 2025 governing use of generative AI by all South Carolina Judicial Branch officers and employees. Policy permits use of approved AI tools only, requiring direct human oversight and approval for drafting documents. AI-generated content may not be used verbatim, assumed truthful, or relied upon as sole source of reference. Policy prohibits using AI to process confidential court records or privileged information unless expressly authorized. Only court-approved AI tools may be used on approved devices. Supervising justices and judges may further limit or prohibit AI use by employees under their supervision. Policy reminds lawyers they remain responsible for accuracy of all work product and must ensure AI use does not compromise client confidentiality or violate Rules of Professional Conduct.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Judicial Branch Officers and Employees may not use Generative AI to draft memoranda, orders, opinions, or other documents without direct human oversight and approval. Generative AI tools are intended to provide assistance and are not a substitute for judicial, legal, or other professional expertise. As such, content from Generative AI may not be used verbatim; be assumed to be truthful, reliable, or accurate; be treated as the sole source of reference; or be solely relied on in making final decisions.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; South Carolina Supreme Court Chief Justice Order effective March 25, 2025

rules.court: Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission
rules.title: Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission Generative AI Guidelines for Judges
rules.summary: Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission published Generative AI Guidelines in October 2025. Non-binding guidelines emphasize keeping human in loop with judge as decision-maker, positioning AI as tool for judges not substitute for judicial judgment. Judges must not delegate to AI the evaluation of briefs, assessment of evidence, or resolution of legal questions. Permissible uses include summarizing legal texts, assisting with document drafting and review, conducting legal research, managing case files, and generating training materials. Non-permissible uses include autonomous decision-making, providing legal analysis without independent human verification, and processing sensitive information without safeguards. Guidelines caution against using free large language models like ChatGPT or Google AI Studio for judicial work, recommending enterprise-level tools with strong security, privacy, and compliance features.
rules.verbatim_key_language: AI is a tool for judges, not a substitute for judicial judgment. The guidelines make clear that judges must not delegate to AI the evaluation of briefs, the assessment of evidence, or the resolution of legal questions.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Louisiana Supreme Court Technology Commission Generative AI Guidelines published October 2025

rules.court: Delaware Supreme Court
rules.title: Interim Policy on the Use of Generative AI by Judicial Officers and Court Personnel
rules.summary: Delaware Supreme Court adopted interim policy in October 2024 governing use of generative AI by judicial officers and court personnel. Policy neither prohibits nor requires use of generative AI. Any judicial officer using generative AI remains responsible for output and must ensure AI-derived content is accurate and aligns with responsibilities. Users must be trained in technical capabilities and limitations of approved GenAI before use. Policy emphasizes AI should not influence judicial decisions or replace human judgment. Creates category of Approved GenAI tools vetted by court administrative office. AI use must conform to existing laws and policies. Delaware Commission on Law and Technology monitors implementation and reports proposed changes as GenAI usage expands.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Delaware Supreme Court interim policy adopted October 2024

rules.court: Utah Judicial Council
rules.title: Utah Judicial Council Interim Rules on Generative AI
rules.summary: Utah Judicial Council implemented interim rules effective October 25, 2023 regarding use of generative AI tools for court-related work or on court-owned devices. Judges and court employees must recognize limitations of generative AI and may not rely solely on AI-generated content. Rules require court personnel complete court-approved training prior to use and disclose any use of generative AI tools to judicial officers. Rules warn that generative AI tools have been known to produce outputs that inadvertently promote stereotypes, reinforce prejudices, or exhibit unfair biases. Emphasizes need for human oversight and verification of all AI-generated content.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Utah Judicial Council interim rules effective October 25, 2023

rules.court: Hawaii Supreme Court
rules.title: Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts Final Report
rules.summary: Hawaii Supreme Court Chief Justice Mark Recktenwald established Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts in April 2024, chaired by Justice Vladimir Devens and Judge John Tonaki. Committee issued final report December 16, 2025 finding existing rules provide adequate safeguards for AI use. Report explores ways legal professionals can use AI tools with training and human oversight for document processing, creating court transcripts and minutes, and providing information to self-represented litigants and non-English speakers. Recommends forming oversight committee to manage AI technology development ensuring consistency with judiciary mission and values. Emphasizes guardrails must be considered with implementation of any future AI technology and human oversight required before implementing or purchasing new AI technology.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; Hawaii Supreme Court Committee on AI and Courts Final Report issued December 16, 2025

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona
rules.title: Standing Order on Use of Generative AI
rules.summary: Judge Soto's order at docket item 15: "IT IS ORDERED as follows: If any portion of a pleading or other document filed on this Court's docket has been drafted (in whole or in part) using generative artificial intelligence, including, but not limited to ChatGPT, Harvey.AI, or Google Bard, all attorneys and pro se litigants filing such pleadings or other documents shall verify that any language that was generated in any form by AI was checked for accuracy by using print reporters, traditional legal databases, or other reliable means by a human being." Signed by Judge James A Soto on 9/1/2023.
rules.provenance: user-provided CourtListener docket URL 2026-04-20; one-off correction

rules.court: Supreme Court of Pennsylvania
rules.title: Interim Policy on Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Pennsylvania Supreme Court adopted interim policy on generative AI in December 2025. Policy allows judges and court staff to use AI tools with appropriate guardrails. Directed at courts, not litigants.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04-20; user correction: moved to guidance_for_courts (about court staff not litigants)

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Kansas
rules.title: Standing Order 26-01: Use of Artificial Intelligence in Preparing Court Filings
rules.summary: "Courts are seeing an increasing number of filings that include false statements of fact or law, including citations to fabricated or incorrect legal authority, which may have been generated by using artificial intelligence ... Litigants are responsible for the content of their own filings and for complying with all applicable procedural rules and duties of candor even when they use AI to generate all or portions of court filings." The order requires certifying that the filer personally reviewed and verified the accuracy of each legal authority cited for any filing that was drafted or assisted by an AI tool.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Litigants are responsible for the content of their own filings and for complying with all applicable procedural rules and duties of candor even when they use AI to generate all or portions of court filings.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1

rules.court: Rhode Island Supreme Court
rules.title: Executive Order No. 2024-03: Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts
rules.summary: Rhode Island Supreme Court Chief Justice Paul A. Suttell issued Executive Order No. 2024-03 establishing a Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts (CAIC) to examine the impact of artificial intelligence technology on the practice of law, the administration of justice, access-to-justice issues, and to inform recommendations on court rules and procedures. The committee was required to submit a written report to the Chief Justice by June 30, 2025.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1, state high-yield sweep

rules.court: Vermont Judiciary
rules.title: Vermont Judiciary Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts - First Annual Report
rules.summary: The Vermont Judiciary Committee on Artificial Intelligence and the Courts released its First Annual Report concluding that amendments to the rules are not warranted, but that guidance for the bar and for judges is appropriate. The report addresses competence requirements for lawyers (keeping abreast of technology benefits) and guidance for judges on appearance of impropriety and understanding risks and benefits of using generative AI when performing judicial responsibilities.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1, state high-yield sweep

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Texas
rules.title: General Order 2025-04: Use of Generative AI in Court Filings
rules.summary: Chief Judge Randy Crane of the Southern District of Texas issued a general order reminding legal practitioners that they are responsible for the accuracy of court filings, even with AI assistance. The order addresses whether generative artificial intelligence drafted any portion of filings and references Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(c), which provides for sanctions if Rule 11(b) has been violated.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri
rules.title: Self-Represented Litigants Guidance on Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: The Eastern District of Missouri provides guidance stating that by presenting to the Court any pleading, written motion, or other paper, self-represented parties acknowledge they will be held responsible for its contents, including any portion generated with artificial intelligence. The district-wide prohibition on generative AI usage applies only to pro se litigants, referencing Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b).
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia
rules.title: Standing Order Regarding Use of Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Judge J. P. Boulee of the Northern District of Georgia issued a standing order requiring attorneys to disclose whether they have used generative AI in the preparation of any document submitted to the court. If AI was used, the filing attorney must certify that all text, citations, and legal authorities have been reviewed for accuracy and that the document is accurate.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia
rules.title: Standing Order Regarding Disclosure of Use of Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Judge Tiffany R. Johnson's standing order requires all counsel and pro se parties to disclose the use of artificial intelligence in any capacity to prepare documents submitted to the Court. If AI was used in any way to prepare a filing or submission to the Court or chambers, counsel and/or pro se parties are required to sign and file a Disclosure of Use of Artificial Intelligence.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas
rules.title: Mandatory Certification Regarding Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "All attorneys appearing before the Court must file on the docket a certificate attesting either that no portion of the filing was drafted by generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey.AI, or Google Bard) or that any language drafted by generative artificial intelligence was checked for accuracy, using print reporters or traditional legal databases, by a human being." Judge Starr's order was the first standing order on AI issued by a federal judge. The order notes that AI platforms "in their current states are prone to hallucinations and bias."
rules.verbatim_key_language: All attorneys appearing before the Court must file on the docket a certificate attesting either that no portion of the filing was drafted by generative artificial intelligence (such as ChatGPT, Harvey.AI, or Google Bard) or that any language drafted by generative artificial intelligence was checked for accuracy, using print reporters or traditional legal databases, by a human being.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania
rules.title: Civil Practice Order on the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "Any party who uses generative AI must include a Certificate of Use of Generative AI that discloses: 1) The specific AI tool that was used; 2) The portions of the filing prepared by the AI program; and 3) That a person has checked the accuracy of any portion of the document generated by AI, including all citations and legal authority." Failure to comply with this order may result in sanctions.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Any party who uses generative AI must include a Certificate of Use of Generative AI that discloses: 1) The specific AI tool that was used; 2) The portions of the filing prepared by the AI program; and 3) That a person has checked the accuracy of any portion of the document generated by AI, including all citations and legal authority.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04, pass 1
